answer:
**In Cynewulf's *Elene* (a single-chapter Old English poem), the True Cross is discovered buried among three crosses on Calvary, but Judas (who led Elene's expedition there) does not initially know which is the correct one, as all three were used in Christ's Crucifixion and those of the two thieves.**[^1]

**To identify it, the crosses are erected publicly in Jerusalem until the ninth hour, when a crowd brings a dead youth on a bier. Judas tests them one by one: the first two have no effect on the corpse. When the third—the true victory-beacon—is raised over the body, the youth immediately revives, his body and soul reunited.**[^2]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

> Then a multitude came, no small folk, and a dead man they brought  
> on a bier in a throng of men nearby  
> (it was then the ninth hour), a youth, spiritless.  
> Then Judas was in his mind-spirit  
> greatly gladdened. He commanded them to set down  
> the soulless one, the life-departed body  
> on the earth, the unliving, and he, a champion of right,  
> raised up two of the roods in his arms,  
> the deep-thinking, over that fated house.  
> It was dead as before, the corpse fast in its lair.  
> The limbs grew cold, covered in death-pangs.  
> Then the third was raised, the holy one.  
> The body was in waiting until upon it  
> the prince’s Rood was raised,  
> the Sky-King’s beam, the true victory-beacon.  
> He at once arose, with spirit endowed,  
> together both body and soul.[^2]
